title: The ethics of remembering people the way they wanted to be remembered
summary: Argue whether the living are obliged to honour the self-image a person wished to leave behind.
topic: The ethics of remembering people the way they wanted to be remembered
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) exploring whether we have an ethical duty to remember the dead — or the absent — in the way they wished to be remembered, or whether honest memory sometimes takes precedence. Use a cleft sentence, a concession ('Granted, …' or 'Admittedly, …'), at least one passive construction, and a participle clause. Include at least one concrete example (e.g. a public figure whose reputation was revised after death, or a family member who asked to be remembered in a particular way). Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What the dead cannot do is defend the version of themselves that history…
Admittedly, there is something uncomfortable about revising a person's story once they can no longer…
Bound by loyalty, many families maintain a carefully edited account of…
It is the living, not the dead, who must ultimately decide…

dung hòa sự thật với lòng trung thành

sampleText:
When a person dies, they leave behind two things: the life they actually lived, and the life they hoped to be remembered for. In many cases those two versions overlap comfortably. In others, they do not. What we choose to do with the gap between them is one of the quieter ethical questions the living are left to resolve.

The case for honouring a person's wished-for memory is not trivial. People invest their whole lives in constructing a self — choosing what to share, what to conceal, what to emphasise — and there is a reasonable argument that this effort deserves respect even after death. A woman who spent forty years building a reputation as a reliable and principled person might plausibly feel that one failure, committed in desperation, does not define her. To foreground that failure in every account of her life is to let a single data point override a far richer story. Being remembered primarily as the sum of one's worst moments is a fate most people would find deeply unjust.

Admittedly, the line between respectful remembrance and hagiography is not always clear. Families are naturally inclined to protect the deceased's image, and the result is often a sanitised version of a person that serves the mourners' emotional needs more than it serves truth. Biographies commissioned by devoted admirers have a tendency to airbrush inconvenient facts, and public statues are routinely built around a mythologised figure rather than the complicated human being who actually stood there.

Even so, I think the duty to remember honestly outweighs the duty to remember loyally — not because truth is more important than kindness, but because a whitewashed memory is ultimately a diminished one. The Nobel laureate who harboured prejudices alongside her brilliance does not become less extraordinary when those prejudices are acknowledged; she becomes more human, and therefore more instructive. History that has been edited to remove all shadow leaves us with no one to learn from: only saints, and saints are not useful guides to the moral choices ordinary people face.

What the dead cannot do is revise their own record in the light of new evidence. That responsibility falls to the living, and meeting it well requires both fairness and courage: the fairness to hold the whole person in view, and the courage to say clearly what was there, even when it was not flattering. Remembering people as they wished to be remembered is an act of love. Remembering them as they were is an act of respect. The more durable tribute, in most cases, is the second one.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to foreground an ethical question
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What we choose to do with the gap between them is one of the quieter ethical questions the living are left to resolve' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' để nhấn mạnh vào chủ đề thực sự của bài luận — không phải cái chết mà là câu hỏi đạo đức mà người còn sống phải đối mặt — bằng cách đặt nó ở vị trí có trọng lượng nhất trong câu.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What we choose to do with the gap between them is one of the quieter ethical questions the living are left to resolve' uses a what-cleft to foreground the essay's real subject — not death itself but the ethical question it leaves for the living — by placing it in the most emphatic position.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Participle clause to compress background information
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Being remembered primarily as the sum of one's worst moments is a fate most people would find deeply unjust' dùng mệnh đề phân từ hiện tại ('Being remembered …') làm chủ ngữ. Cách này nén một khái niệm phức tạp thành một cụm từ duy nhất và cho phép câu đi thẳng đến phán xét giá trị ('a fate … deeply unjust').
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Being remembered primarily as the sum of one's worst moments is a fate most people would find deeply unjust' uses a present participle clause as subject. This compresses a complex concept into a single phrase and allows the sentence to move directly to its value judgement.

sampleTranslationVi:
Khi một người qua đời, họ để lại hai thứ: cuộc sống họ thực sự đã sống, và cuộc sống họ hy vọng được ghi nhớ. Trong nhiều trường hợp, hai phiên bản này chồng lên nhau một cách thoải mái. Trong những trường hợp khác thì không. Điều chúng ta chọn làm với khoảng cách giữa chúng là một trong những câu hỏi đạo đức lặng lẽ hơn mà người còn sống được giao lại để giải quyết.

Lý lẽ cho việc tôn trọng ký ức mà người ta mong muốn không phải là không đáng kể. Người ta dành cả cuộc đời để xây dựng bản thân — chọn lựa điều gì để chia sẻ, điều gì để che giấu, điều gì để nhấn mạnh — và có lý lẽ hợp lý rằng nỗ lực này xứng đáng được tôn trọng ngay cả sau khi qua đời. Một người phụ nữ đã dành bốn mươi năm xây dựng danh tiếng là người đáng tin cậy và có nguyên tắc có thể có lý khi cảm thấy rằng một thất bại duy nhất, thực hiện trong tuyệt vọng, không xác định bà ấy. Được nhớ đến chủ yếu như là tổng hợp của những khoảnh khắc tồi tệ nhất là một số phận mà hầu hết mọi người sẽ thấy là vô cùng bất công.

Thừa nhận rằng, ranh giới giữa sự tưởng nhớ tôn trọng và tiểu sử tô hồng không phải lúc nào cũng rõ ràng. Các gia đình tự nhiên có xu hướng bảo vệ hình ảnh của người đã khuất, và kết quả thường là một phiên bản đã được làm sạch của một người phục vụ nhu cầu cảm xúc của những người đang để tang hơn là phục vụ sự thật. Tiểu sử được ủy thác bởi những người hâm mộ tận tụy có xu hướng xóa bỏ những sự thật bất tiện, và các bức tượng công cộng thường xuyên được xây dựng xung quanh một nhân vật đã được huyền thoại hóa thay vì con người phức tạp thực sự đã đứng ở đó.

Dẫu vậy, tôi nghĩ nghĩa vụ nhớ một cách trung thực vượt trội hơn nghĩa vụ nhớ một cách trung thành — không phải vì sự thật quan trọng hơn lòng tốt, mà vì một ký ức bị tô trắng xét cho cùng là một ký ức bị thu nhỏ. Nhà khoa học đoạt giải Nobel ôm chứa những định kiến bên cạnh tài năng của bà không trở nên kém phi thường hơn khi những định kiến đó được thừa nhận; bà trở nên nhân bản hơn, và do đó hữu ích hơn. Lịch sử đã được chỉnh sửa để loại bỏ mọi bóng tối không để lại cho chúng ta ai để học hỏi: chỉ có những vị thánh, và các vị thánh không phải là hướng dẫn hữu ích cho những lựa chọn đạo đức mà người bình thường phải đối mặt.

Điều người chết không thể làm là sửa đổi hồ sơ của chính họ dưới ánh sáng của bằng chứng mới. Trách nhiệm đó thuộc về những người đang sống, và thực hiện tốt trách nhiệm đó đòi hỏi cả sự công bằng lẫn lòng dũng cảm: sự công bằng khi nhìn nhận toàn bộ con người, và lòng dũng cảm để nói rõ ràng những gì đã có ở đó, ngay cả khi nó không nịnh hót. Nhớ đến mọi người như họ mong muốn được ghi nhớ là một hành động yêu thương. Nhớ đến họ như họ thực sự là là một hành động tôn trọng. Lời tưởng nhớ bền lâu hơn, trong hầu hết các trường hợp, là cái thứ hai.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a 'what' cleft to introduce the essay's central ethical question?
options: What we choose to do with the gap between them is one of the quieter ethical questions the living are left to resolve.
It is what we choose the gap between them the quieter ethical questions left resolve.
The gap between them is what the ethical questions are what we choose to resolve.
What the living is resolve the quieter ethical questions gap between them we choose.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What we choose to do with the gap … is …' is the grammatically correct what-cleft, placing the key noun phrase at the end for emphasis.
mcQuestions.hint: The cleft structure is 'What [subject+verb phrase] is [noun phrase]'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'hagiography' mean in this context?
options: A scientific study of death and mourning rituals
An excessively flattering account that ignores a person's faults
A legal document specifying how a person wishes to be buried
A formal history written by a professional biographer
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Hagiography' originally referred to the lives of saints; it now broadly means any biography that is uncritically adulatory.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what saints' biographies traditionally omit.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'admittedly' correctly as a concession marker?
options: Admittedly, the line between respectful remembrance and hagiography is not always clear.
Admittedly the hagiography line between remembrance respectful clear always.
The line, admittedly it is, between remembrance clear and hagiography.
Admittedly always the line between clear hagiography not remembrance is.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Admittedly, [full clause]' is the correct structure for a concession opener.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the option where 'admittedly' is followed by a complete, grammatically correct clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction correctly foregrounds what is done to a person's reputation?
options: People often airbrush inconvenient facts from a biography.
Inconvenient facts are often airbrushed out of a biography.
Biographies airbrush the inconvenient facts people often.
Often the biography airbrushed inconvenient facts people.
mcQuestions.explanation: The passive 'are often airbrushed out' puts 'inconvenient facts' in subject position, foregrounding what is being lost rather than who removes it.
mcQuestions.hint: Which sentence makes 'inconvenient facts' the grammatical subject?

options: A memory that has been painted on a white wall as a memorial
A memory that has been selectively cleaned up to remove anything negative
A memory that is too painful to be shared with others
A memory preserved in a formal written record
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Whitewash' means to cover up faults or to present only a favourable picture; a whitewashed memory therefore hides flaws.
mcQuestions.hint: What does the verb 'whitewash' do to something unpleasant?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase best describes a participle clause used as a sentence subject?
options: 'Being remembered primarily as the sum of one's worst moments is a fate most people would find deeply unjust.'
'A fate is that most people remembered primarily the worst moments deeply unjust.'
'Most people is a fate that being remembered the worst moments unjust.'
'Deeply unjust people being remembered the worst moments is a fate primarily.'
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Being remembered …' is a present participle clause acting as the grammatical subject of the sentence, followed by the main verb 'is'.
mcQuestions.hint: The subject of the sentence is an -ing phrase, not a noun.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase means 'the right to control how your own story is told'?
options: posthumous reputation
curated memory
autonomy over one's story
contested legacy
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Autonomy' refers to self-governance; 'autonomy over one's story' means the power to decide how one's life is narrated.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase contains the word meaning 'self-rule' or 'self-determination'?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'outweigh' correctly in a comparison of duties?
options: The duty to remember honestly outweighs the duty to remember loyally.
The duty honestly outweighed remembering the loyally duty.
Remembering honestly outweigh the duty loyally the duty.
Outweighs the honestly duty the loyally duty remembering.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'X outweighs Y' means X is more important or significant than Y; this is the standard comparison structure.
mcQuestions.hint: Subject + 'outweighs' + object is the correct form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best pivots from a concession back to the main argument?
options: In summary,
For instance,
Even so, I think the duty to remember honestly outweighs the duty to remember loyally.
In other words,
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' acknowledges what was just conceded and pivots back with a stronger claim — a classic concession-rebuttal structure.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase signals that despite what was just admitted, the main point still holds?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to mythologise' a person mean?
options: To write an academic article about their cultural significance
To turn them into an almost legendary, idealised figure that obscures their real complexity
To argue that their story is not historically accurate
To make their achievements known to a wider international audience
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Mythologise' means to treat someone as a myth-like, larger-than-life figure, stripping away human complexity.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what myths do to historical figures compared to realistic biography.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains a formal academic register throughout?
options: Dead people can't speak up anymore so basically we can say whatever about them.
The deceased's wishes deserve consideration, but honest remembrance ultimately serves a broader social purpose.
It's kind of sad when people die and nobody bothers to remember them right.
Like, honouring someone's memory is cool but also truth matters a lot too right?
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The deceased's wishes', 'honest remembrance', and 'broader social purpose' are all precise, formal collocations appropriate to academic argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid contractions, slang, and sentence-final question tags.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which word means 'a person who has recently lost a loved one through death'?
options: the deceased
the bereaved
the mourner's legacy
the heir
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The bereaved' refers specifically to those left behind by a death; 'the deceased' is the person who died.
mcQuestions.hint: Which noun refers to the surviving people affected by the death, not the person who died?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'That responsibility falls to the living' mean?
options: The living accidentally drop responsibility on the ground
The responsibility of honest remembrance is something the living — not the dead — must take on
The living are frightened by the responsibility of remembering
Responsibility is a concept that applies only to living people, not institutions
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Falls to someone' is an idiom meaning it becomes their duty or burden to handle something.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Falls to' is an idiom — what does it mean for a duty to 'fall to' someone?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a relative clause to add information without interrupting the main clause?
options: The Nobel laureate who harboured prejudices alongside her brilliance does not become less extraordinary when those prejudices are acknowledged.
The Nobel laureate does not become less extraordinary prejudices harboured alongside brilliance when acknowledged.
Alongside brilliance the Nobel laureate prejudices harboured does not become acknowledged less extraordinary.
Prejudices alongside brilliance the Nobel laureate does not become who harboured acknowledged less.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'who harboured prejudices alongside her brilliance' is a defining relative clause giving essential information about which laureate is meant.
mcQuestions.hint: The relative clause begins with 'who' and immediately follows the noun it modifies.

options: An inheritance that is being disputed in a court of law
A person's record that different people evaluate very differently, often with disagreement
A legacy that is so well-known it cannot be argued with
A reputation that has been legally protected after death
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Contested' means disputed or disagreed upon; a contested legacy is one that scholars, the public, or family members interpret in conflicting ways.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'contested' as 'fought over' — what is being fought over?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best concludes an essay by contrasting two acts and clearly preferring one?
options: So basically remembering people is complicated and there are two sides.
Remembering people as they wished to be remembered is an act of love. Remembering them as they were is an act of respect. The more durable tribute, in most cases, is the second one.
In conclusion, both sides have valid points and we should think carefully about this topic.
Remembering people is important and so is truth so both matter equally.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B uses parallel structure and a precise distinction to land on a clear position — the hallmark of a strong argumentative conclusion.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option ends with a definite preference rather than a vague balance?

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a well-known figure — historical or contemporary — whose reputation has been revised after death. Was that revision fair, or did it erase something important? What does your answer reveal about whose needs posthumous memory serves?
